headline.title: Grammarly’s ‘expert review’ is just missing the actual experts
headline.summary: A recently-added feature in Grammarly purports to improve users’ writing with help from the world's great writers and thinkers — and some tech journalists, too.

tools.title: The OpenClaw superfan meetup serves optimism and lobster
tools.summary:
The woman at the door wore a plush lobster headdress. 
She sat in the front hallway of a multistory event venue in Manhattan, beside a bundle of wristbands. If she granted you one, the world of ClawCon beckoned behind her - full of vibey pink and pur
tools.content:
The woman at the door wore a plush lobster headdress. 
She sat in the front hallway of a multistory event venue in Manhattan, beside a bundle of wristbands. If she granted you one, the world of ClawCon beckoned behind her - full of vibey pink and purple lighting, lobster claw headbands, multicolored name tags, sponsor information stations, and a demo stage underneath a skylight. Hundreds of people were gathered to celebrate OpenClaw, the AI assistant platform created by Peter Steinberger in Nove

creative.summary: New US guidelines for AI contracts would force companies to grant the government an irrevocable license for "all lawful use" and ban ideological bias in AI outputs, which is itself an ideological requirement with striking parallels to China's approac
creative.content:
New US guidelines for AI contracts would force companies to grant the government an irrevocable license for "all lawful use" and ban ideological bias in AI outputs, which is itself an ideological requirement with striking parallels to China's approach.

creative.content:
When large language models hallucinate, they leave measurable traces in their own computations. Researchers at the Sapienza University of Rome have developed a training-free method that picks up on these traces and generalizes better than previous approaches.

creative.title: Anthropic's Claude Code subscription may consume up to $5,000 in compute per month while charging the user just $200
creative.summary: Anthropic's $200 Claude Code subscription could consume up to $5,000 in compute per user, according to Cursor's internal analysis reported by Forbes. The numbers reveal just how aggressively AI companies are subsidizing their coding tools and what th
creative.content:
Anthropic's $200 Claude Code subscription could consume up to $5,000 in compute per user, according to Cursor's internal analysis reported by Forbes. The numbers reveal just how aggressively AI companies are subsidizing their coding tools and what that could mean for prices once these tools become essential.
The article Anthropic's Claude Code subscription may consume up to $5,000 in compute per month while charging the user just $200 appeared first on The Decoder.

culture.content:
Tech policy professor who served in US air force explains how a feud between an AI startup and the US military illuminates ethical fault lines
Anthropic’s ongoing fight with the Department of Defense over what safety restrictions it can put on its artificial intelligence models has captivated the tech industry, acting as a test of how AI may be used in war and the government’s power to coerce companies to meet its demands.

culture.title: ‘It means missile defence on datacentres’: drone strikes raise doubts over Gulf as AI superpower
culture.summary:
Iran’s targeting of commercial datacentres in the UAE and Bahrain signals a new frontier in asymmetric warfare
It is believed to be a first: the deliberate targeting of a commercial datacentre by the armed forces of a country at war.
At 4.30am on Sun
culture.content:
Iran’s targeting of commercial datacentres in the UAE and Bahrain signals a new frontier in asymmetric warfare
It is believed to be a first: the deliberate targeting of a commercial datacentre by the armed forces of a country at war.
